edges.description: Loss of SLC3A1 impairs renal cystine and dibasic amino acid reabsorption.
Loss of PREPL contributes to congenital hypotonia, weakness, ptosis, and feeding problems.
PREPL loss contributes to growth hormone deficiency, early poor growth, and later hyperphagia or obesity.
The contiguous deletion presentation includes variable developmental, neurobehavioral, and dysmorphic features.
High urinary cystine concentration promotes crystallization and nephrolithiasis.
Defective cystine and dibasic amino-acid reabsorption produces the diagnostic cystinuria phenotype.
Urinary amino-acid testing detects increased cystine from failed proximal tubular reabsorption.
Cystine stones produce renal and urinary tract morbidity.
Neuromuscular transmission impairment produces early generalized hypotonia.
Bulbar or generalized weakness can impair neonatal feeding.
PREPL-related myasthenic transmission impairment produces muscle weakness.
Ocular muscle involvement in the PREPL-related myasthenic syndrome produces ptosis.
PREPL deficiency is associated with growth hormone deficiency in HCS and isolated PREPL deficiency.
Clinically apparent cystine stones manifest as nephrolithiasis.
Early feeding and growth impairment manifests as failure to thrive.
PREPL deficiency is associated with growth hormone deficiency.
Growth retardation and growth hormone deficiency contribute to short stature.
Later childhood hyperphagia follows the early poor-feeding period in some HCS patients.
PREPL deficiency can produce childhood obesity after the early feeding/growth phase.
Xerostomia is part of the broader PREPL deficiency phenotype.
Variable neurodevelopmental involvement can include global developmental delay.
Minor facial dysmorphism is a reported HCS feature without a resolved intermediate mechanism.
